courses.title: Machine Design II — Machine Elements
courses.summary: The rotating-equipment bill of materials designed properly: gears, rolling and journal bearings, belts and chains, clutches, brakes, and couplings — every element a maintenance engineer has replaced, now sized from first principles.
taught_from: MIT OCW 2.72 Elements of Mechanical Design

lessons.scope: Lewis bending analysis with velocity and geometry factors. Module and face width chosen against tooth breakage. Where root cracks start and why.
Contact stress by Hertzian logic; pitting as the surface fatigue mode. Bending versus pitting as competing failure budgets. The design meaning of gear micropitting found in an oil sample.
Helical geometry and axial thrust; bevel basics; worm-drive efficiency and its heat problem. Selection logic across gear types. Thrust-bearing consequences of helix choices.
Bearing types and the L10 life equation used honestly. Equivalent loads with combined radial and thrust. Fits, preload, and mounting — where installed life is won or lost.

Hydrodynamic action from Reynolds-equation reasoning in outline; Sommerfeld thinking and minimum film thickness. Viscosity selection and clearance. The bearings under every large turbine and compressor.
V-belt mechanics from the capstan relation; power ratings and service factors. Tensioning — the maintenance variable that decides belt life. Multi-belt sets and matching.
Roller-chain geometry and chordal action. Ratings, lubrication regimes, and elongation limits. Sprocket wear read as a diagnostic.
Friction clutch and brake torque from pressure assumptions — uniform wear versus uniform pressure derived. Energy dissipation and thermal sizing. Where brakes fade and clutches slip.

Rigid, elastomeric, gear, and disc couplings; what misalignment each tolerates and transmits. Coupling selection against torque and misalignment spectra. The design basis under alignment standards used in the field.
Rotating-disc stresses and burst margins. Flywheel energy sizing revisited with stress limits. Overspeed as a design case, not an accident report.

lessons.t: Design review: a gearbox teardown on paper
lessons.scope: A complete gearbox audited element by element: gears, bearings, shafts, seals, housing. Each element's failure budget identified. The design-side twin of a maintenance teardown report.

courses.title: Control Systems I
courses.summary: Feedback made quantitative: modeling, transfer functions, stability, root locus, frequency response, and the PID controller — the theory under every temperature loop and speed governor in the plant.
taught_from: Ogata-tradition control theory via MIT OCW 2.04A Systems and Controls (ocw.mit.edu)

lessons.scope: Mechanical, thermal, and fluid systems reduced to ODEs. Analogies across domains made explicit. The model as a decision about what to ignore.
From ODE to transfer function; poles and zeros as the system's anatomy. Block-diagram algebra. The s-plane as a map of behavior.

Time constants, damping ratio, natural frequency, and the step-response vocabulary: rise time, overshoot, settling. Reading system character from a trend chart. The response shapes every operator recognizes without naming.
Feedback's gifts: disturbance rejection and desensitization — and its price: potential instability. Error constants and system type. Why integral action exists.
The stability question posed algebraically. Routh arrays and parameter ranges for stability. Gain limits computed before anything oscillates in the field.
Pole migration with gain drawn by rule. Design by pole placement on the locus. Seeing why more gain destabilizes — graphically, permanently.
Sinusoidal steady state; magnitude and phase plots constructed from asymptotes. Gain and phase margins as robustness currency. The frequency view that unifies control and vibration analysis.
P, I, and D actions each derived from a need; tuning by Ziegler–Nichols and by informed iteration. Integral windup and derivative noise handled practically. The controller that runs 90% of industry.
Actuators, positioners, and valve characteristics inside the loop. Deadband, stiction, and hysteresis as control killers. Loop problems that masquerade as tuning problems.
Sampling in the loop; the PLC/DCS as a discrete controller. Scan-rate effects on stability margins. Enough discrete theory to respect the digital reality.
Speed, tension, and temperature loops from real machinery contexts — an extruder barrel and a winder analyzed. Loop interaction previewed. Control theory attached to hardware nameplates.

courses.title: PLC II & Industrial Networks
courses.summary: Beyond single-rack thinking: structured programming, analog and PID in the PLC, fieldbuses and industrial Ethernet, drives integration, and the networked architecture of a modern line.

lessons.scope: Function blocks, structured text, and sequential function charts beside ladder. Choosing a language per task. Code organization for machines with modes.
Modes, states, and transitions designed before coding. Auto/manual/maintenance mode architecture. The pattern that makes complex machines debuggable.

lessons.t: Analog processing and PID in the PLC
lessons.scope: Scaling, filtering, and alarm limits on analog channels. The PLC PID instruction configured and tuned. Temperature and flow loops closed in logic.
Why networks replaced point wiring; topology, addressing, and determinism. Modbus RTU/TCP as the lingua franca. Reading a network architecture drawing.
EtherNet/IP and PROFINET in outline; switches, VLANs, and segmentation for machine networks. Diagnostics from port LEDs to managed-switch counters. When the 'PLC fault' is a network fault.
Drive parameters, control words, and status words over the network. Speed and torque references from logic. Commissioning a networked drive without folklore.
Arrays, structures, and recipe management for product changeovers. Batch parameters downloaded safely. Production data captured at source for OEE.
Alarm philosophy applied in logic: priority, deadband, and state-based suppression. First-out capture for trips. Designing the diagnostics you will want during the outage.
Why plant networks are attacked and how they are segmented, patched, and backed up. Remote access done with adult supervision. The maintenance engineer's cyber hygiene responsibilities.
Two PLC stations, one network, shared interlocks, one HMI: specify, program, and fault-test the pair. Handshake design under failure conditions. The systems view PLC I could not yet teach.

courses.title: Hydraulics & Pneumatics
courses.summary: Fluid power for people who will maintain it: pumps, valves, actuators, and circuits read from schematics; pressure, flow, and force sized; and the contamination control that decides hydraulic life.
taught_from: NPTEL: Fundamentals of Industrial Oil Hydraulics and Pneumatics (nptel.ac.in)

lessons.scope: Pascal's law to force multiplication; power as pressure times flow. Where fluid power beats electromechanical drives. The efficiency price paid for force density.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Oil Hydraulics and Pneumatics

lessons.scope: Fluid functions and properties; ISO cleanliness codes read and specified. Filtration placement and beta ratios. Contamination as the leading cause of hydraulic failure — treated with due respect.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Oil Hydraulics and Pneumatics

lessons.scope: Gear, vane, and piston pumps; fixed versus variable displacement. Volumetric efficiency measured and trended. Pressure-compensated control explained without mystery.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Oil Hydraulics and Pneumatics

lessons.scope: Cylinder sizing for force and speed; cushioning and seal packages. Hydraulic motors in brief. Rod buckling and mounting classes — statics put to work.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Oil Hydraulics and Pneumatics

lessons.scope: Relief, reducing, and sequence valves; flow controls and meter-in/meter-out logic. Cracking pressure and override characterized. The valves that shape a circuit's personality.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Oil Hydraulics and Pneumatics

lessons.scope: Spool centers and their consequences; ISO 1219 symbols read fluently. Standard circuits: regenerative, counterbalance, unloading. Schematic-to-machine correspondence drilled.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Oil Hydraulics and Pneumatics

lessons.scope: Gas-charged accumulators: sizing, precharge, and safety. Shock suppression and supplementary flow. Stored energy respected during maintenance.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Oil Hydraulics and Pneumatics

lessons.scope: Heat, noise, and slow motion as the three complaints; a diagnostic tree for each. Test-point measurements against the schematic. Case-based practice on press and mobile circuits.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Oil Hydraulics and Pneumatics

lessons.scope: Air preparation — filter, regulator, lubricator; valve and cylinder families; sizing with compressibility acknowledged. Where pneumatics wins: speed, cleanliness, simplicity. Leakage economics revisited from Thermo II.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Oil Hydraulics and Pneumatics

lessons.scope: Solenoid valves, reed sensors, and PLC-driven pneumatic sequences. Grippers and pick-and-place motions. The actuation layer of packaging machinery.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Oil Hydraulics and Pneumatics

lessons.t: Proportional and servo systems first contact
lessons.scope: Proportional valves and closed-loop positioning in outline. Where servo-hydraulics still rules: presses and test rigs. The control-systems course connected to oil.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Industrial Oil Hydraulics and Pneumatics

courses.title: Welding Engineering & NDT
courses.summary: The joint as an engineered product: weld metallurgy and the HAZ, defects and their acceptance, procedure qualification (WPS/PQR), and the NDT methods — PT, MT, UT, RT — that decide what ships. Kirby and HEISCO territory.
taught_from: Groover, Fundamentals of Modern Manufacturing — joining chapters
Kalpakjian & Schmid, Manufacturing Engineering and Technology

lessons.scope: The weld thermal cycle: peak temperatures and cooling rates across the joint. Fusion zone, HAZ, and base metal as three different materials. Why welding is heat treatment you didn't order.
lessons.src: Groover, welding chapters; Callister & Rethwisch, Ch. 10

lessons.scope: HAZ sub-zones mapped to the Fe–C diagram; hardening, softening, and grain growth located. Carbon equivalent, preheat, and hydrogen control as the cold-cracking triangle. The study guide every welding interview draws from.
lessons.src: Callister & Rethwisch, Ch. 9–10; Groover, welding chapters
preview: A carbon equivalent of 0.48 on 25 mm plate triggers which two precautions, and against which cracking mechanism?
Which HAZ sub-zone hardens most, and what two variables set its final hardness?
Why does thicker plate cool a weld faster, and what does that do to martensite risk?

lessons.scope: Why welds pull: thermal contraction against restraint. Distortion modes and sequencing countermeasures. PWHT — what it relieves and what it costs.

Porosity, lack of fusion, undercut, cracks hot and cold, inclusions — each with its cause chain and its radiographic/ultrasonic signature. Discontinuity versus defect: acceptance criteria as the deciding line. WPS/PQR logic: qualifying a procedure, then policing it.
preview: On a radiograph, how does lack of fusion look different from porosity?
Discontinuity versus defect: what single thing turns the first into the second?
WPS versus PQR: which is the tested evidence, which the working recipe, and in what order do they exist?

lessons.t: Welding symbols and joint design review
lessons.scope: Symbol fluency on fabrication drawings; effective throat and size selection recalled from design. Access and position as quality factors. Reading a Kirby-style shop drawing's weld map.
Carbon and low-alloy steels, stainless (sensitization and ferrite control), and aluminum's oxide and porosity problems. Dissimilar joints in outline. Matching filler to base with reasons.

lessons.t: NDT principles and the methods matrix
lessons.scope: PT, MT, UT, and RT: physical principle, what each detects, surface versus volumetric reach, and access/geometry limits — condensed into the selection matrix inspectors actually use. Personnel qualification levels demystified. Which method finds which weld defect, and why.
preview: A surface-breaking crack in painted ferromagnetic steel: PT or MT, and why?
Why can RT miss a tight planar crack that UT finds easily?
Pick methods: subsurface porosity in a 12 mm aluminum weld versus a surface fatigue crack on a shaft.

lessons.scope: Wave propagation, probes, and calibration blocks. Angle-beam weld scanning geometry. Sizing and characterizing indications — where UT skill actually lives.
Source selection, geometry, and image quality indicators. Film and digital reading of the classic defect appearances. Radiation safety as an absolute boundary.

WPS variables — essential and nonessential; PQR testing: tensiles, bends, macros. Welder performance qualification and its limits of position and thickness. The paperwork that makes welds trustworthy.
Arc gouging and defect excavation; repair sequences that don't make things worse. Excavation verification by MT/PT. Repair welding as controlled surgery, permitted and documented.
Assemble an inspection and test plan for a welded assembly: hold points, NDT extents, acceptance criteria, and records. The QA/QC document flow of a fabrication contract walked once end to end.

courses.title: Maintenance Engineering Fundamentals
courses.summary: The discipline you came from, rebuilt as engineering: failure behavior over time, the P–F interval, maintenance strategy types, and the vocabulary — availability, MTBF, criticality — used precisely instead of loosely.
taught_from: NPTEL: Maintenance and reliability-related engineering courses (nptel.ac.in)
Practitioner curriculum structured against the SMRP CMRP body-of-knowledge domains (framework, not a textbook)

lessons.scope: Failure as a process, not an event: load versus capability over time. Failure modes, mechanisms, and causes distinguished rigorously. The language that makes failure discussable.
lessons.src: SMRP CMRP framework; NPTEL maintenance courses

lessons.t: Failure patterns and the P–F curve
lessons.scope: The six classic failure patterns and the uncomfortable evidence that most failures are not age-related. The P–F curve derived as a concept: potential failure, functional failure, and the inspection window between them. The single most consequential diagram in modern maintenance.
lessons.src: SMRP CMRP framework; RCM literature as surveyed in NPTEL reliability courses
preview: Classic studies found most failure modes are not age-related: what does that do to time-based overhauls?
Define P and F on the P–F curve, and state the rule for a legitimate inspection interval between them.
A defect is detectable ~3 months before functional failure: what is the longest defensible monitoring interval?

lessons.scope: Run-to-failure, time-based, condition-based, and proactive strategies — each legitimate somewhere. Strategy selection by failure pattern and consequence. Why 'more PM' is not a strategy.
MTBF, MTTR, availability, and utilization defined with equations and abused-examples. Inherent versus operational availability. Calculating them from real work-order data, including the data's lies.
lessons.src: SMRP CMRP framework; NPTEL reliability courses

lessons.scope: Ranking equipment by consequence and likelihood; criticality matrices built honestly. The 10% of assets that deserve 60% of engineering attention. Criticality as the master key to every later prioritization.
PM tasks written against failure modes, not habits. Intervals from failure data and P–F logic. The PM review: deleting tasks as a legitimate outcome.
Lubrication regimes from journal-bearing theory; grease versus oil decisions. Selection, storage, and contamination control. Lubrication as the cheapest reliability program in the plant.

Alignment, balancing, fastener torque, and installation standards as failure-prevention engineering. The craftsmanship–reliability link quantified where possible. Why 'fitted and ran' is not a completion standard.
CLAIR-style operator tasks: clean, lubricate, adjust, inspect, report. The operations–maintenance interface designed, not fought over. TPM origins acknowledged without the cult.
Cost of downtime versus cost of maintenance; direct, indirect, and consequence costs. The optimum that moves with criticality. Making a maintenance budget argument management can audit.

lessons.t: The maintenance function in the organization
lessons.scope: Roles: technician, planner, supervisor, reliability engineer — who owns what. Centralized versus area maintenance. Where the transitioning maintenance engineer's experience already maps onto this course's language.
